findings.title: getPendingForValidator: unbounded findMany — no limit/pagination for ADMIN path
findings.description: When the calling user has `leaves:manage_any` (ADMIN / RESPONSABLE), `getPendingForValidator` issues an unbounded `findMany` on the entire `leaves` table, filtered only by status. No `take` or pagination parameter is applied. A large organisation with many concurrent pending/cancellation-requested leaves can return thousands of rows, each joined with user, leaveType and validator sub-selects. The service-perimeter path (lines 965-1011) has the same gap.
findings.root_cause: The function returns the Prisma promise directly without wrapping it in a safeLimit guard, unlike `findAll` which has `Math.min(limit, 500)`.
findings.impact: An ADMIN calling GET /leaves/pending-validation can receive an arbitrarily large response, causing high memory usage on both the API process and the PostgreSQL server; response times grow linearly with the number of pending leaves.
findings.suggested_fix: Add pagination parameters (`page`, `limit`) to the method signature, apply `Math.min(limit, 100)` as the cap, and return a paginated `{ data, meta }` envelope consistent with `findAll`.
acceptance_criteria: GET /leaves/pending-validation with default parameters returns at most 100 rows
A `meta.total` field is present in the response
ADMIN with 500 pending leaves gets paginated results, not all 500 at once
findings.notes: Both the hasManageAny branch (line 896) and the hasApprove branch (line 965) are unbounded.

findings.title: getOwnLeaves: unbounded findMany on leave history — no limit
findings.description: GET /leaves/me returns every leave ever created for the calling user, with no pagination or `take` limit. A user who has been in the organisation for multiple years can accumulate hundreds of leave records. Each row includes joins on user, leaveType, validator, and validatedBy.
findings.root_cause: No `take` parameter is passed to `findMany`; the method has no pagination contract.
findings.impact: Long-tenured users experience slow responses on /leaves/me; payload size can exceed 100 KB in large datasets, adding unnecessary serialisation overhead.
findings.suggested_fix: Add optional `page`/`limit` parameters; default to most recent N (e.g. 50) leaves or restrict to current year by default with an opt-in `all` parameter.
acceptance_criteria: GET /leaves/me returns at most 50 rows by default
A `meta.total` allows the frontend to paginate further

findings.title: expandRecurringRulesForRange: N+1 findUnique+create inside nested loop over days × rules
findings.description: For each active rule, for each calendar day in the requested range that matches the rule's weekday, the code issues one `findUnique` query and potentially one `create` query. With R rules and a 30-day window, this is up to R×5 `findUnique` calls (one per weekday occurrence). Since `findAll` calls this method on every read request that includes a date range, this N+1 fan-out fires on every listing call, not just during schedule generation.
findings.root_cause: The existence check is done row-by-row inside a sequential async loop instead of bulk-loading existing schedules for the entire (userIds × date range) and computing the missing set in memory before issuing batched inserts.
findings.impact: A GET /telework?startDate=2026-01-01&endDate=2026-06-30 for an organisation with 20 users and 3 recurring rules each triggers ~180 sequential DB round-trips before the actual data query executes; median response time grows from ~5 ms to several hundred milliseconds under load.
findings.suggested_fix: Pre-load all existing `TeleworkSchedule` rows for the (ruleUserIds × date range) with a single `findMany`, store them in a `Set<string>` keyed on `${userId}|${dateKey}`, then collect missing dates and issue a single `createMany` (or batched `$transaction`).
acceptance_criteria: GET /telework with a 6-month date range issues at most 2 DB queries (load rules + load existing schedules) before inserting missing rows
Correctness: same schedules are materialised as before
findings.notes: Same pattern exists in generateSchedulesFromRules (finding P3-4) and findForPlanningOverview also calls expandRecurringRulesForRange.

findings.title: generateSchedulesFromRules: same N+1 findUnique+create loop as expandRecurringRulesForRange
findings.description: POST /telework/recurring-rules/generate replicates the same sequential findUnique + create loop. Materialising a 6-month window for 30 users with 3 rules each involves ~1350 individual DB round-trips (one findUnique per matching day per rule). The issue is identical in structure to finding P3-3 but in an explicit admin endpoint, where the user controls the date range (bounded only by the DTO, which has no documented upper limit on the range length).
findings.root_cause: No bulk existence check; each day is probed independently within a sequential `for` loop.
findings.impact: POST /telework/recurring-rules/generate with a large date range (e.g. full year) can take tens of seconds and block the Node.js request worker for the entire duration, preventing other requests from being served concurrently.
findings.suggested_fix: Same as P3-3: bulk-load existing schedules for all (userId × date range) combinations with one `findMany`, compute missing slots in memory, then insert via `createMany` or chunked `$transaction([...creates])`.
acceptance_criteria: POST /telework/recurring-rules/generate for a 1-year range issues O(rules) queries for lookups + 1 bulk insert, not O(rules × weekday occurrences) individual queries
Created/skipped counts remain accurate

findings.title: importLeaves: getHolidayKeySet + findValidatorForUser called per row inside transaction loop
findings.description: Inside the `importLeaves` transaction loop (one iteration per CSV row), two multi-query DB operations are called per row: (1) `getHolidayKeySet` issues a `holiday.findMany` for each row's date range; (2) `findValidatorForUser` issues 1–3 queries (user + delegate lookup + possible role fallback) per user. For a 100-row CSV, this means 100+ holiday queries and up to 300+ validator queries — all serialised inside a single long-running Postgres transaction. The comment on line 3558 acknowledges this as 'lecture par ligne' but frames it as acceptable for 'volume d'import modéré', which is not enforced.
findings.root_cause: No pre-computation of the holiday set covering the entire CSV span before the loop, and no memoisation of validator lookups per userId across rows.
findings.impact: A 200-row CSV import can hold a Postgres transaction open for 10–30+ seconds, blocking VACUUM and other writers on the `leaves` table. The `errorDetails` array in the result also grows unboundedly.
findings.suggested_fix: 1. Compute a single holiday key set covering `min(startDate)..max(endDate)` of the entire CSV once before the loop (already done for the span filter — extend it to the holiday pre-fetch). 2. Memoize `findValidatorForUser` per userId: `const validatorCache = new Map<string, string|null>()` and call `findValidatorForUser` only on the first occurrence of each userId. 3. Consider enforcing a CSV row limit (e.g. 500) in the DTO.
acceptance_criteria: A 100-row CSV triggers at most 1 `holiday.findMany` call total (not 100)
A 100-row CSV with 10 distinct users triggers at most 10 `findValidatorForUser` calls (not 100)
findings.notes: The CSV import DTO has no `@Max()` on the array length, making payload size user-controlled.

findings.title: getLeaveBalance: Promise.all fan-out issues N×2 DB queries, one resolveAllocatedDays per leave type
findings.description: `getLeaveBalance` loads all active leave types, then fans out into `Promise.all(leaveTypes.map(...))` where each iteration calls `resolveAllocatedDays` — which itself issues 2 sequential queries (individual balance findUnique + global balance findFirst). With N leave types (e.g. 8), this fires 16 concurrent DB queries. While concurrent rather than sequential, this adds per-request DB connection pressure and latency tail. The worker comment (PER-002) documents the bulk allLeaves approach but `resolveAllocatedDays` still fans out.
findings.root_cause: `resolveAllocatedDays` is not batch-aware: it always issues 2 queries for a single (userId, leaveTypeId, year) triple. There is no bulk variant that resolves all types for a user in a single query.
findings.impact: GET /leaves/me/balance issues ~16 DB queries per call (for 8 leave types). At high traffic, this multiplies connection pool usage by ×16 compared to a single bulk query.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `resolveAllocatedDaysForAll(userId, leaveTypeIds, year)` variant that uses `leaveBalance.findMany({ where: { userId, leaveTypeId: { in: leaveTypeIds }, year } })` and `leaveBalance.findMany({ where: { userId: null, leaveTypeId: { in: leaveTypeIds }, year } })` — two queries total, mapped to a Record<leaveTypeId, days> in memory.
acceptance_criteria: GET /leaves/me/balance issues at most 4 DB queries regardless of the number of leave types (1 allLeaves + 2 bulk balance lookups + 1 leaveTypes list)
findings.notes: Lower priority than P3-3/P3-4 because the fan-out is bounded by the number of leave types (typically < 15) and the queries run in parallel.

findings.title: holidays findAll: unbounded findMany — no limit, no pagination
findings.description: GET /holidays returns all holiday rows with no limit. In practice this table is small (~11 rows per year × years in DB), but there is no upper bound enforced at code level. If an organisation retains many years of history and imports recurring holidays, this could grow to hundreds of rows returned on every call.
findings.impact: Low impact currently (holidays table is bounded by ~11 rows/year × N years), but the endpoint lacks the defensive cap that other list endpoints apply.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an optional `year` filter (already provided by `findByYear`) to the `findAll` controller endpoint, or add a hard cap of `take: 500`.
acceptance_criteria: GET /holidays with no query param returns at most 500 rows

findings.title: findAll school-vacations: unbounded findMany — no pagination for year-scoped list
findings.description: GET /school-vacations with no `year` filter returns all school vacation records across all years. The table grows with each import cycle (each zone × year adds up to ~10 rows). Without a limit, an organisation with several years of data will return all records unboundedly.
findings.root_cause: No `take` clause; the `year` filter is optional with no fallback cap.
findings.impact: Low current impact (table stays small), but the endpoint is missing the standard defensive cap pattern.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `take: 200` as a hard cap or require the `year` query parameter (set it as required in the controller or default to current year).
acceptance_criteria: GET /school-vacations returns at most 200 rows

findings.title: findAll leaves: default limit 1000 with effective cap 500 — higher than recommended 100
findings.description: The function signature defaults `limit` to 1000, but the effective cap is `Math.min(limit || 500, 500)`. Callers passing `limit=1000` silently receive 500 rows. The default of 500 is 5× the 100-row checklist ceiling; each row includes 4 joined objects (user, leaveType, validator, validatedBy). At 500 rows with 4 joins, the response payload can easily exceed 100 KB in a real organisation.
findings.root_cause: The cap of 500 was chosen without documenting the payload size risk; no upper limit is documented in the API contract.
findings.impact: An admin-level user calling GET /leaves without filters can receive a 500-row response per page; at scale (large organisation, many leaves) this creates excessive serialisation time and network bandwidth usage.
findings.suggested_fix: Lower the default to 50 and the cap to 100. Add the `safeLimit` cap documentation to the Swagger description.
acceptance_criteria: GET /leaves with no limit param returns at most 50 rows
GET /leaves?limit=999 returns at most 100 rows
meta.totalPages is correct
findings.notes: time-tracking findAll has the same issue: safeLimit = Math.min(limit || 1000, 1000) at time-tracking.service.ts:363.

findings.title: findAll time-tracking: effective cap is 1000 rows — far exceeds recommended 100
findings.description: The time-tracking `findAll` method allows callers to retrieve up to 1000 time entries per page. Each entry includes user, thirdParty, declaredBy, task, and project joins. At 1000 rows with 5 joins, the serialised payload can approach 500 KB for entries with verbose descriptions.
findings.root_cause: No documented rationale for the 1000-row cap; likely carried over from an earlier iteration without review.
findings.impact: A MANAGER calling GET /time-tracking without date filters for a large project can receive a multi-hundred-KB response that degrades both server and client performance.
findings.suggested_fix: Lower the default to 50 and cap to 100, consistent with best practice.
acceptance_criteria: GET /time-tracking with no limit returns at most 50 rows
GET /time-tracking?limit=2000 returns at most 100 rows

findings.title: leave-types reorder: N individual update queries instead of bulk upsert
findings.description: `reorder` issues one `UPDATE` statement per leave type inside an interactive transaction. For N leave types, this produces N round-trips within a single Postgres transaction. While the table is typically small (< 20 rows), the pattern does not scale and adds unnecessary Postgres statement overhead. A `CASE`-based bulk update or raw SQL `UPDATE … SET sortOrder = CASE …` would accomplish this in a single statement.
findings.root_cause: Prisma does not expose a native multi-row `updateMany` with per-row values; the code maps to individual updates without using a raw query alternative.
findings.impact: Negligible at current scale (< 20 leave types); becomes noticeable if the leave type count grows into the hundreds.
findings.suggested_fix: Use `prisma.$executeRaw` with a `CASE`-based UPDATE, or if admin-only and small-scale, accept the current approach but cap the input array size (e.g. `@MaxLength(50)` on the DTO).
acceptance_criteria: Reordering 10 leave types issues at most 2 DB statements (1 batch UPDATE + 1 SELECT for findAll)

findings.title: TeleworkSchedule: no index on (userId, date) range queries — only unique constraint
findings.description: The `TeleworkSchedule` model (schema line 733) declares `@@unique([userId, date])` but no separate `@@index([userId, date])`. Postgres creates a B-tree index on the unique constraint, which IS usable for range queries on `(userId, date)`. However, `findForPlanningOverview` queries `userId: { in: [...new Set(userIds)] }, date: { gte, lte }` — an IN list on userId with a date range. The unique constraint index on `(userId, date)` would be used for single-user lookups, but for multi-userId IN list queries Postgres may choose a bitmap index scan or sequential scan instead depending on cardinality. The schema has no composite index optimised for this access pattern.
findings.root_cause: The `@@unique` serves integrity, not query performance for multi-user range scans; no dedicated range index exists.
findings.impact: Medium: `findForPlanningOverview` with many userIds (planning view for 30+ users) may perform poorly without an index on (date, userId) that favours the date-range-first access pattern.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `@@index([date, userId])` to `TeleworkSchedule` to complement the unique constraint, optimising the date-range-first queries used in planning overview.
acceptance_criteria: EXPLAIN ANALYZE on `SELECT * FROM telework_schedules WHERE user_id IN (...) AND date BETWEEN ? AND ?` shows an Index Scan not a Seq Scan for tables with > 10k rows
findings.notes: The Postgres query planner may already use the unique index effectively for small tables; this finding applies at scale.

verified_clean: leaves.service.ts findAll: paginated with safeLimit=Math.min(limit,500), returns {data, meta}
leaves.service.ts getAvailableDays: correct select projection (only needed fields), bounded by userId+leaveTypeId+year filter
leaves.service.ts hasConfiguredBalance: uses select:{id:true} — minimal projection, not over-fetching
leaves.service.ts resolveAllocatedDays: uses findUnique (exact key lookup) + findFirst with narrow filter — no scan
leaves.service.ts validateLeavesImport: pre-computes date span filter (PER-009) — existingLeaves scoped to CSV window only
leaves.service.ts importLeaves: pre-computes importSpanFilter to bound existingLeaves query
leaves.service.ts getLeaveBalance: bulk single-query allLeaves (PER-002 comment), in-memory grouping by leaveTypeId
leaves.service.ts getBalances: optional year/userId filters applied before findMany, bounded by those filters
leaves.service.ts getDefaultBalances: userId=null filter applied, bounded by year filter
leaves.service.ts getManagedUserIds: uses distinct:['userId'] on userService to avoid duplicate userId explosion
leaves.service.ts getServiceIds: batches managedServices + userServices with Promise.all (2 queries), has memo support
time-tracking.service.ts getUserReport: groupBy pushes aggregation to Postgres, no JS-side reduce over raw rows
time-tracking.service.ts getProjectReport: all aggregation done via groupBy/aggregate — 6 parallel Postgres aggregations, no raw-row iteration
time-tracking.service.ts findAll: paginated with safeLimit, returns {data, meta}
time-tracking.service.ts getUserEntries: date-range filter applied before findMany, no unbounded query
holidays.service.ts findByRange: scoped to date range, no unbounded query
holidays.service.ts findByYear: scoped to year date range
holidays.service.ts importFrenchHolidays: fixed 11-record loop — not user-controlled N; each create errors caught for P2002
holidays.service.ts countWorkingDays: single findByRange + in-memory day iteration — no per-day DB query
school-vacations.service.ts findByRange: scoped to date range overlap filter
school-vacations.service.ts importFromOpenData: records deduplicated in-memory before individual inserts; max 50 API records (hardcoded limit param)
leave-types.service.ts findAll: reference table, small and bounded, includes only _count aggregate
telework.service.ts findAll: paginated with safeLimit (default 50 param, cap 1000 — see P3 issue), returns {data, meta}
telework.service.ts findAllRecurringRules: scoped by userId unless manage_any, no unbounded global scan for non-admin paths
telework.service.ts getWeeklySchedule: scoped to 7-day window — bounded query
telework.service.ts getUserStats: scoped to single year — bounded query
telework.service.ts getTeamSchedule: scoped to single date — bounded query
leave payload projections: all findMany include blocks use select:{} to project only needed fields — no select:* over-fetching

leaves.service.ts checkOverlap: uses findMany rather than findFirst but result length only checked >0; could be findFirst — minor but not a performance regression risk given userId+date overlap filter
